concepts.title: Newton's equations and conserved quantities
concepts.blurb: For three point masses interacting by gravity, Newton's second law gives $\ddot r_i = G\sum_{j\ne i} m_j (r_j-r_i)/|r_j-r_i|^3$. The $18$-dimensional phase space carries ten classical integrals — total energy $E$, momentum $P$ (3), centre-of-mass $R$ (3), and angular momentum $L$ (3) — leaving $8$ effective degrees of freedom after Jacobi reduction.

concepts.title: Restricted three-body problem & Lagrange points
concepts.blurb: When one mass is negligible, the heavy pair traces a Keplerian orbit and the test particle sees a time-independent potential in the rotating frame. The effective potential $U_{\mathrm{eff}}=-\tfrac12\Omega^2(x^2+y^2)-Gm_1/r_1-Gm_2/r_2$ has five critical points $L_1$–$L_5$: three collinear saddles ($L_1,L_2,L_3$) and two equilateral maxima ($L_4,L_5$) which become Lyapunov-stable when $m_1/m_2 > (25+3\sqrt{69})/2 \approx 24.96$.

concepts.title: Lagrange, Euler, and the figure-eight
concepts.blurb: The full three-body problem admits a thin family of explicit solutions. Lagrange (1772) found the equilateral configuration: three masses at the vertices of an equilateral triangle that rotates rigidly. Euler (1767) gave the collinear family with the three masses on a rotating line in fixed ratios. Centuries later Chenciner and Montgomery (2000) proved the existence of the figure-eight: three equal masses chasing each other along a single planar curve with the symmetry of the lemniscate.

concepts.title: Poincaré's tangle — the birth of chaos
concepts.blurb: Poincaré's 1889 prize memoir, in correcting an error, discovered that the stable and unstable manifolds of a hyperbolic periodic orbit can intersect transversally — and once they do, they must do so infinitely often, weaving a homoclinic tangle of unimaginable complexity. Smale later distilled this into the horseshoe map: a topological mechanism producing infinitely many periodic orbits and sensitive dependence on initial conditions. The three-body problem is where dynamical systems was born.

concepts.title: KAM theorem and stability of the solar system
concepts.blurb: Kolmogorov (1954), Arnold (1963), and Moser (1962) showed that for a small Hamiltonian perturbation $H = H_0(I) + \varepsilon H_1(I,\theta)$ of an integrable system, most invariant tori (those whose frequencies satisfy a Diophantine condition $|k\cdot\omega|\ge \gamma|k|^{-\tau}$) survive — only the resonant ones break up into Birkhoff zones. The set of surviving tori has positive measure tending to full measure as $\varepsilon\to 0$, giving long-time stability of the planetary problem in a measure-theoretic sense (Arnold, Laskar).

concepts.title: Spacecraft trajectories and Trojan asteroids
concepts.blurb: The Lagrange points are real estate: $L_2$ of the Sun–Earth system parks JWST in eternal anti-solar shade, and halo orbits around $L_1$ host SOHO. Gravity assists exploit the energy exchange in close encounters of the restricted problem to slingshot Voyager out of the solar system. Jupiter's $L_4$ and $L_5$ host the Trojan asteroids — over $10^4$ catalogued — librating in the equilateral wells. Exoplanet stability analyses use KAM-style criteria to bound the architectures that can persist for billion-year timescales.
